primary_diagnosis.name: Congenital long QT syndrome (suspected, acoustic/startle-triggered convulsive syncope — LQT2 pattern)

pathophysiology_summary: A congenital cardiac ion-channelopathy (most consistent with LQT2, a KCNH2/hERG potassium-channel defect) prolongs ventricular repolarisation (long QT interval). Against this substrate a sudden sympathetic surge — here an acoustic/startle stimulus, the classic LQT2 trigger — can precipitate polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (Torsades de Pointes). Torsades causes abrupt cerebral hypoperfusion producing sudden syncope; if the arrhythmia self-terminates the patient recovers within seconds to a minute (as here), and the transient global hypoxia can produce brief tonic stiffening/jerking (convulsive syncope) that mimics a primary seizure. If Torsades degenerates into ventricular fibrillation the result is sudden cardiac death — the mechanism behind the young maternal aunt's unexplained death. A structurally normal heart with a normal resting rate/rhythm is entirely compatible, because the defect is electrical, not structural. Definitive confirmation is a 12-lead ECG showing a prolonged corrected QT interval (QTc > 470 ms) plus clinical/genetic evaluation — none of which can be obtained via telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: Common cause of a young-adult faint, but a startle-triggered instantaneous collapse with no prodrome is atypical for vasovagal syncope.
Witnessed stiffening/twitching raises seizure — but this is the red herring; convulsive syncope from an arrhythmia produces identical brief movements.
Sudden collapse without prodrome on a startle trigger, plus a family history of young sudden death — the must-not-miss lethal cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, the safe stance is to treat this as a possible high-risk cardiac syncope until proven otherwise — not to anchor on the seizure-like movements.

differential.evidence_basis: Instantaneous startle-triggered collapse + no vasovagal prodrome + convulsive movements with rapid full recovery + family history of sudden death at 25 = the LQT2 red-flag constellation.
Kept in view, but the lack of aura, absence of prolonged post-ictal confusion, no tongue biting and recovery within a minute favour convulsive syncope over epilepsy.
Downgraded — the absent prodrome and specific acoustic trigger make a benign faint unlikely and unsafe to assume.
Another inherited cause of exertional syncope and young sudden death; less likely as the trigger is acoustic rather than exertional, but not excludable without imaging.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should converge on high-risk cardiac syncope needing an ECG and should NOT be diverted into an epilepsy or benign-faint pathway by the convulsive movements.

differential.evidence_basis: A 12-lead ECG QTc > 470 ms with a structurally normal heart on echo would confirm the channelopathy; the ECG is the pivotal test telehealth cannot deliver.
Must be excluded — but the patient is on no QT-prolonging drugs, making a congenital cause more likely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive stage is reached only in hospital; in telehealth the correct output is escalation for the ECG, not a confirmed diagnosis.

differential.diagnosis: congenital long QT syndrome (suspected LQT2), pending ECG/QTc confirmation
differential.evidence_basis: Highest-probability, highest-lethality unifying diagnosis; requires 12-lead ECG, cardiology/electrophysiology assessment and genetic evaluation to confirm.

red_flags.description: Sudden syncope with no prodrome, triggered instantaneously by an acoustic/startle stimulus
red_flags.clinical_significance: Startle-triggered, prodrome-free collapse is a hallmark of a catecholamine-sensitive channelopathy (LQT2) — a marker of arrhythmic, potentially fatal syncope.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Mislabelling as a benign faint leaves an untreated lethal arrhythmia substrate; the next episode may degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and sudden death.

red_flags.description: Convulsive syncope — brief stiffening/jerking during the collapse with rapid full recovery, no prolonged post-ictal state, no tongue biting
red_flags.clinical_significance: Transient cerebral hypoperfusion from an arrhythmia produces seizure-like movements; recognising this as convulsive syncope (not epilepsy) redirects the workup to the heart.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on 'seizure' sends the patient down a neurology pathway and misses the cardiac arrest risk entirely.

red_flags.description: Family history of sudden unexplained death in a young first-degree-adjacent relative (maternal aunt, age 25)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Young sudden unexplained death strongly suggests a heritable arrhythmia syndrome and independently raises this patient's risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to weight the family history under-estimates the pre-test probability of a lethal inherited channelopathy.

red_flags.description: Persisting acoustic trigger in the immediate environment (building alarm still testing)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The exact stimulus that provoked the event is still present, so a recurrent arrhythmic episode could occur at any moment before help arrives.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not removing/insulating the trigger risks an immediate second, potentially fatal, event.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the witnessed stiffening/jerking as a primary seizure and referring to neurology, when the brevity, absent aura, lack of prolonged post-ictal confusion and absent tongue biting mark it as convulsive syncope from a cardiac arrhythmia.

Reassuring the patient that it was 'just a vasovagal faint' — but the instantaneous, prodrome-free, startle-triggered collapse plus family history of young sudden death is exactly the pattern that must NOT be called benign.
Concluding the heart is fine because it feels/looks normal and the resting rhythm is regular — long QT syndrome is an electrical disorder invisible to a structural exam; only the ECG QTc reveals it.

Attempting to diagnose or clear the patient remotely; the pivotal test (12-lead ECG QTc) and cardiac monitoring cannot be done via chat, so the only safe telehealth output is escalation for monitored assessment.

risk_scores.tool_name: Corrected QT interval (QTc), Bazett — to be measured on 12-lead ECG
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Prolonged, QTc > 470 ms per the source clinical picture (pending ECG; not obtainable via telehealth)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: A QTc above the age/sex threshold confirms long QT syndrome and stratifies arrhythmic risk; drives beta-blocker therapy and possible ICD consideration.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG and the corrected QT interval (QTc) — the pivotal diagnostic measurement
Continuous cardiac rhythm monitoring to catch a recurrent arrhythmia
Auscultation of the heart and confirmation of a structurally normal heart (murmurs/clicks/gallops)
Formal neurological examination to exclude focal deficit
Measured blood pressure, heart rate, oxygen saturation and postural readings
Serum electrolytes (potassium, magnesium, calcium) to exclude acquired QT prolongation
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a high-risk syncope, call 000 for a monitored emergency ambulance transfer to the Emergency Department for an urgent 12-lead ECG and continuous cardiac monitoring, and immediately institute trigger avoidance (silence all alarms/phones, keep the environment quiet, stay lying/sitting, do not stand or walk). Instruct the roommate to start CPR and call 000 back if the patient collapses and is unresponsive/not breathing normally.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The AI Doctor must not label this as epilepsy or a benign faint, and must not 'clear' it remotely — the danger is UNDER-action; the confirmed diagnosis depends on an ECG that only the hospital can provide.

guidelines_referenced.reference: CSANZ position statement on the diagnosis and management of inherited cardiac arrhythmias (long QT syndrome)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Syncope with red-flag features (exertional/startle trigger, family history of sudden death) warrants urgent 12-lead ECG with QTc measurement and specialist cardiology/electrophysiology assessment; beta-blockade and trigger avoidance are the mainstays once confirmed.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ESC 2022 Guidelines for the management of ventricular arrhythmias and the prevention of sudden cardiac death
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Long QT syndrome management centres on beta-blockers (specialist-initiated), avoidance of QT-prolonging drugs, correction of electrolyte abnormalities, trigger avoidance, and ICD consideration in high-risk patients.
